title: CCCS - Generated questions from public CrowdStrike sources
note: Questions in English, explanations in Portuguese. Verified against public crowdstrike.com pages, CrowdStrike GitHub (falcon-helm, falcon-operator, fcs-action) and official blog posts, July 2026.

questions.question: Under the shared responsibility model, which security tasks remain the CUSTOMER's responsibility in a public cloud environment?

options.text: Physical security of the data centers
Securing workloads, data, identities, and cloud service configurations
Maintenance of the hypervisor and underlying hardware
Availability of the cloud provider's global network

questions.explanation: Under the shared responsibility model, the provider secures the cloud infrastructure and the customer secures what they put in it: workloads, data, identities, and configurations.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Physical security is the provider's responsibility.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Configuration, data, identities, and workloads belong to the customer.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Hypervisor and hardware belong to the provider.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Global network availability belongs to the provider.

questions.memory_tip: Provider: security OF the cloud. Customer: security IN the cloud.

questions.question: What best describes a Cloud-Native Application Protection Platform (CNAPP) such as Falcon Cloud Security?

options.text: A firewall appliance deployed at the edge of the cloud network
A unified platform that integrates CSPM, CWP, CIEM, and related capabilities in a single console
A backup and disaster recovery service for cloud workloads
A tool that only scans container images in registries

questions.explanation: CNAPP unifies posture (CSPM), workload protection (CWP), identity (CIEM), and related capabilities in a single integrated platform.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. CNAPP is not a network appliance.
option_explanations.B: Correct. This is the definition of CNAPP: posture + workload + identity unified.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Backup and DR are not part of CNAPP's scope.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Image scanning is just one of the capabilities, not the definition.

questions.memory_tip: CNAPP = CSPM + CWP + CIEM in one place.

questions.question: Why does Falcon Cloud Security combine agentless visibility with the Falcon sensor instead of relying on only one approach?

options.text: Agentless scanning is required to install the sensor
The sensor only works in on-premises environments
Agentless provides broad posture visibility while the sensor adds real-time runtime protection, covering both build and runtime
Agentless scanning replaces the need for any runtime protection

questions.explanation: The combined approach uses agentless for broad posture visibility and the sensor for real-time runtime protection, covering both build and execution.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. They are independent mechanisms.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The sensor protects workloads in the cloud and on-prem.
option_explanations.C: Correct. Each approach covers one part: broad visibility + runtime protection.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Agentless does not replace runtime detection and prevention.

questions.memory_tip: Agentless sees, the sensor protects. Together: complete coverage.

questions.question: Which mechanism is used to register an AWS account in Falcon Cloud Security during onboarding?

options.text: Deploying a CloudFormation template/stack provided by Falcon
Installing the Falcon sensor on every EC2 instance first
Opening a support ticket with CrowdStrike
Enabling AWS GuardDuty in all regions

questions.explanation: AWS account registration uses a CloudFormation template that creates the roles and permissions needed for inventory, real-time visibility, and agentless scanning.

option_explanations.A: Correct. AWS onboarding is done via a CloudFormation stack.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Sensors protect workloads, but do not register the account.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Registration is self-service in the console.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. GuardDuty is an AWS service, not part of Falcon registration.

questions.memory_tip: Register AWS = CloudFormation template.

questions.question: After registering an AWS account, the Real-time visibility and detection status shows a GREEN dot. What does this indicate?

options.text: IOAs are active and all regions are capturing CloudTrail management events
The account has no misconfigurations
All EC2 instances have the Falcon sensor installed
DSPM has classified all sensitive data

questions.explanation: Green indicates a healthy integration: active IOAs and all regions capturing CloudTrail management events. Orange would indicate partial coverage.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Green = active IOAs + complete regional coverage.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The status reflects the integration, not posture.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The indicator does not measure sensor coverage.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. DSPM is a separate capability.

questions.memory_tip: Green: everything active and complete. Orange: active but incomplete.

questions.question: Which capabilities does AWS cloud registration enable in Falcon Cloud Security? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Asset inventory of cloud resources
Real-time visibility and detection (RTVD) of control plane activity
Automatic installation of the Falcon sensor on every workload
Replacement of AWS IAM as the identity provider

questions.explanation: Account registration enables asset inventory and real-time visibility/detection (RTVD), and also serves as the foundation for DSPM and agentless scanning.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Asset inventory is enabled by registration.
option_explanations.B: Correct. RTVD monitors control plane activity via CloudTrail.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Registration does not automatically install sensors.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Falcon does not replace IAM.

questions.memory_tip: Registration enables: inventory + RTVD (and opens the door to DSPM/agentless).

questions.question: What is an Indicator of Misconfiguration (IOM) in Falcon Cloud Security?

options.text: Evidence that an adversary is actively attacking the environment
A risky cloud configuration that exposes the environment, such as a public storage bucket
A malware signature found on a cloud workload
A performance bottleneck in a cloud service

questions.explanation: IOM identifies a risky configuration (a state), such as a public bucket or open port, unlike IOA, which identifies attack behavior.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Active attack behavior is an IOA.
option_explanations.B: Correct. IOM = misconfiguration that exposes the environment.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Malware is a workload detection, not an IOM.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Performance is not part of posture security's scope.

questions.memory_tip: IOM = bad configuration (state). IOA = attack behavior (action).

questions.question: A security team wants to distinguish between a state-based finding and a behavior-based finding in the cloud. Which pairing is correct?

options.text: IOM: static misconfiguration risk · IOA: active adversary behavior
IOM: active adversary behavior · IOA: static misconfiguration risk
Both IOM and IOA describe only compliance violations
IOA is generated only after a breach is confirmed

questions.explanation: IOM assesses the state of the configuration; IOA detects suspicious or malicious behavior occurring in the environment.

option_explanations.A: Correct. IOM is state, IOA is behavior.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The definitions are reversed.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. IOA goes beyond compliance, it detects attacks.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. IOA detects behavior before a breach is confirmed.

questions.memory_tip: M for Misconfiguration (state), A for Attack (action).

questions.question: Which findings are typical cloud misconfigurations detected by CSPM? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Storage bucket exposed to public access
Security group allowing remote administration ports open to the internet
Database with encryption and automated backups enabled
IAM users protected with MFA

questions.explanation: Public buckets and administrative ports open to the internet are classic misconfigurations. Enabled encryption and MFA are best practices, not risks.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Public storage exposure is a classic misconfiguration.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Admin ports open to 0.0.0.0/0 are a risk.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. This is the desired state, not a problem.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Enabled MFA is a best practice.

questions.memory_tip: Misconfiguration = exposure: public, open, unencrypted, no MFA.

questions.question: Against which types of standards does CSPM continuously measure a cloud environment for compliance?

options.text: Only internal company policies
Industry frameworks and benchmarks such as CIS, NIST, PCI DSS, and HIPAA
Only the cloud provider's default settings
Software licensing agreements

questions.explanation: CSPM assesses the environment against industry frameworks and benchmarks, such as CIS, NIST, PCI DSS, HIPAA, and FedRAMP, in addition to custom policies.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Internal policies complement, but are not the only basis.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Recognized industry frameworks are the compliance reference.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Provider defaults are not compliance frameworks.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Licensing is not part of CSPM's scope.

questions.memory_tip: CSPM compliance: CIS, NIST, PCI DSS, HIPAA.

questions.question: How does Falcon Cloud Security help teams FIX a misconfiguration after detecting it?

options.text: It provides guided remediation steps in the detection details
It automatically deletes the misconfigured resource
It opens the cloud provider console and applies changes without review
It only exports a CSV of findings with no guidance

questions.explanation: The platform offers guided remediation: documented steps in the detection's details panel to fix the configuration.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Guided remediation appears in the detection's Remediation section.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Automatically deleting resources would be destructive.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Unreviewed changes are not the default behavior.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Export exists, but there is also remediation guidance.

questions.memory_tip: Detected it? The Remediation section says how to fix it.

questions.question: What is configuration drift in the context of cloud security posture?

options.text: The gradual divergence of deployed resources from their secure baseline due to unauthorized or untracked changes
The migration of workloads between cloud regions
An increase in cloud spending over time
The automatic scaling of resources under load

questions.explanation: Configuration drift is when the environment moves away from the secure baseline due to unauthorized or untracked changes, creating exposure over time.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Divergence from the baseline due to uncontrolled changes.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Region migration is an operation, not posture drift.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Cost is not security posture.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Auto scaling is expected behavior.

questions.memory_tip: Drift = moved away from the baseline with no one controlling it.

questions.question: Why is CSPM described as an agentless capability?

options.text: It requires the Falcon sensor on every cloud resource
It assesses posture through the cloud provider's APIs without installing software on workloads
It only works on containers
It runs exclusively on-premises

questions.explanation: CSPM consumes the provider's APIs (enabled during account registration) to assess configurations, without needing an agent on workloads.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. CSPM does not depend on an installed sensor.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Assessment is via the provider's API, agentless.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. CSPM covers cloud accounts and services, not just containers.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The scope is the cloud.

questions.memory_tip: CSPM reads cloud APIs: no agent.

questions.question: During image assessment, why is it critical to detect secrets such as passwords and API keys inside container images?

options.text: Secrets increase the image size significantly
Developers may leave credentials in images before pushing to registries, exposing them to anyone who pulls the image
Secrets slow down container startup
Registries reject images containing secrets automatically

questions.explanation: Credentials forgotten in images are exposed to anyone with access to the registry, enabling lateral movement and unauthorized access.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Size is not the risk.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Embedded secrets leak with the image's distribution.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. There is no meaningful performance impact.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Registries do not block secrets by default.

questions.memory_tip: Secret in the image = public secret for whoever pulls the image.

questions.question: A team builds application images on top of an external base image from a public registry. What is the main security concern?

options.text: Base images cannot be scanned
Vulnerabilities and malware in the base image are inherited by every derived image
Base images always expire after 30 days
Public registries encrypt images, blocking inspection

questions.explanation: Vulnerabilities and malware present in the base image are inherited by every image built on top of it, multiplying the risk.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Base images can and should be scanned.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Everything in the base propagates to the derived images.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. There is no automatic expiration.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Public images can be inspected.

questions.memory_tip: A vulnerable base image contaminates the whole family of images.

questions.question: What does container drift detection identify at runtime?

options.text: A container running software or binaries that were not present in the original image
A container consuming more CPU than requested
An image stored in more than one registry
A pod scheduled on the wrong node

questions.explanation: Drift indicates the running container has diverged from the original image, for example new binaries written at runtime, a strong signal of compromise.

option_explanations.A: Correct. A change relative to the original immutable image is drift.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. CPU consumption is an operational matter, not security.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Replication across registries is not drift.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Scheduling is Kubernetes' responsibility.

questions.memory_tip: A container should be immutable. Changed at runtime? Drift.

questions.question: A Kubernetes Admission Controller policy is configured in prevent mode. What happens when a non-compliant workload is submitted to the cluster?

options.text: The workload is deployed and flagged for later review
The workload is blocked from being admitted to the cluster
The workload is deployed with reduced privileges automatically
The cluster is shut down as a precaution

questions.explanation: In prevent mode, the Admission Controller blocks admission of the workload that violates the policy, before it runs.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Deploy with a later flag describes audit/alert mode.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Prevent mode = blocked at admission.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The KAC does not rewrite the workload's privileges.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Disproportionate action, not the actual behavior.

questions.memory_tip: KAC prevent: stopped at the cluster's door.

questions.question: What is the purpose of connecting container registries to Falcon Cloud Security?

options.text: To host application source code
To automatically assess stored images for vulnerabilities and misconfigurations
To replace the registry's authentication system
To compress images and reduce storage costs

questions.explanation: Connecting registries lets Image Assessment automatically evaluate stored images, finding vulnerabilities, secrets, and misconfigurations.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Registries store images, not source code, and Falcon does not host them.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Connected registry = images assessed automatically.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Authentication remains the registry's own.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. There is no compression function.

questions.memory_tip: Connect the registry = scan images where they live.

questions.question: What is the goal of Infrastructure as Code (IaC) scanning in a shift-left strategy?

options.text: To find misconfigurations in templates such as Terraform or CloudFormation BEFORE resources are deployed
To scan running cloud resources after deployment
To convert manual configurations into code
To measure the cost of infrastructure changes

questions.explanation: IaC scanning identifies security issues in templates before deployment, preventing misconfigurations from reaching production.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Shift left: fix it in the code, before deployment.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Already-deployed resources are CSPM's scope.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Converting configuration into code is IaC adoption, not scanning.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Cost is not the goal of security scanning.

questions.memory_tip: IaC scanning: catch the error in the template, not in production.

questions.question: Where should container image assessment be integrated to catch vulnerabilities as early as possible?

options.text: Only in production, after workloads are running
In the CI/CD pipeline, during the build phase
Only during quarterly security audits
In the cloud provider's billing console

questions.explanation: Integrating assessment into the CI/CD pipeline (build phase) allows blocking vulnerable images before they reach the registry and the cluster.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Production is too late for the first barrier.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Build in CI/CD is the earliest, most automatable point.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Periodic audits do not keep pace with deployment speed.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Billing is unrelated to assessment.

questions.memory_tip: Image shift left: scan at CI/CD build.

questions.question: What does a verified image policy enforce in Falcon Cloud Security?

options.text: That only approved and assessed images can progress through the pipeline and run in clusters
That all images must be smaller than 1 GB
That images can only be built on Fridays
That every image must use the latest tag

questions.explanation: Verified image policies ensure that only approved and assessed images progress through the pipeline and run on hosts or Kubernetes clusters.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Only a verified image runs.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Size is not a policy criterion.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. There is no calendar restriction.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Using latest is actually a versioning bad practice.

questions.memory_tip: Verified image policy: no approval, no run.

questions.question: Which two types of scanning does the Falcon Cloud Security (FCS) CLI and its CI integrations support in the pipeline?

options.text: IaC misconfiguration scanning and container image vulnerability scanning
Network packet capture and DNS filtering
Endpoint memory forensics and disk imaging
Load testing and performance profiling

questions.explanation: The FCS CLI and CI integrations (such as GitHub Actions) support IaC scanning for misconfigurations and container image scanning for vulnerabilities.

option_explanations.A: Correct. IaC + image are the two scopes of shift left in the pipeline.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Packet capture is not part of the FCS CLI's scope.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Endpoint forensics is a different domain of the platform.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Performance is not pipeline security.

questions.memory_tip: FCS in the pipeline: scans the template (IaC) and the image.

questions.question: Why must the Falcon sensor for Linux run as a privileged container when deployed to Kubernetes nodes?

options.text: It requires access to the node's kernel to monitor and protect all workloads on the node
Privileged mode makes the sensor start faster
Kubernetes requires all DaemonSets to be privileged
It needs to modify the container images stored on the node

questions.explanation: The node sensor works at the host kernel level, which requires privileged execution, and that's why it covers every pod on the node via DaemonSet.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Kernel access requires privilege and gives full visibility of the node.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. It is not a matter of speed.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. DaemonSets do not need to be privileged by default.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The sensor does not alter images.

questions.memory_tip: Node sensor = kernel = privileged = DaemonSet.

questions.question: Which characteristics describe the Falcon Container sensor for Linux? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Runs as an unprivileged container in user space
Is injected as a sidecar into application pods
Requires kernel module installation on the worker node
Only works on Windows nodes

questions.explanation: The Container sensor runs without privilege, in user space, and is injected as a sidecar into application pods, ideal where there is no access to the node.

option_explanations.A: Correct. It does not run code in the node's kernel.
option_explanations.B: Correct. The deployment model is a sidecar per pod.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. It is exactly the opposite: it does not touch the kernel.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. It is a sensor for Linux.

questions.memory_tip: Container sensor: user space + sidecar, no kernel access.

questions.question: In which scenario should you choose the Falcon Container sensor instead of the Falcon sensor for Linux as a DaemonSet?

options.text: When you have full root access to all cluster nodes
When the environment does not allow node access or privileged containers, such as managed serverless Kubernetes
When the cluster runs on bare-metal servers you own
When you want kernel-level visibility of the worker node

questions.explanation: Without node access or permission for privileged containers (managed/serverless environments), the option is the Container sensor as a sidecar.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. With node access, DaemonSet is recommended.
option_explanations.B: Correct. No node access = unprivileged sidecar.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Owned bare-metal allows the node sensor.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Kernel-level visibility is exactly what the sidecar does NOT have.

questions.memory_tip: Can't touch the node? Container sensor (sidecar).

questions.question: An Amazon EKS cluster runs both EC2 worker nodes and Fargate pods. What is the recommended approach for full sensor coverage?

options.text: Deploy the Falcon sensor for Linux as a DaemonSet on EC2 nodes and the Falcon Container sensor as a sidecar for Fargate pods
Deploy only the DaemonSet; it also covers Fargate pods
Deploy only the Container sensor on EC2 nodes
Fargate pods cannot be protected at all

questions.explanation: Mixed environments combine both: DaemonSet on EC2 nodes (kernel access) and Container sensor via sidecar on Fargate pods (no node access).

option_explanations.A: Correct. Each node type gets the appropriate sensor.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. DaemonSet does not reach Fargate pods (there is no accessible node).
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. On EC2 with node access, the DaemonSet gives better coverage.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Fargate is covered by the Container sensor.

questions.memory_tip: Mixed EKS: DaemonSet on EC2 + sidecar on Fargate.

questions.question: Which tools does CrowdStrike provide to deploy and manage Falcon sensors in Kubernetes at scale?

options.text: Helm charts and the Falcon Operator
Manual kubectl exec into each pod
A browser extension
FTP upload to each node

questions.explanation: CrowdStrike maintains Helm charts and the Falcon Operator to deploy and manage sensors in Kubernetes clusters in a standardized, scalable way.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Helm and Operator are the official methods.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. A manual process does not scale and is not supported.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. There is no browser extension for this.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. FTP is not a sensor deployment method.

questions.memory_tip: K8s at scale: Helm chart or Falcon Operator.

questions.question: What runtime telemetry should container protection capture to support detection and investigation in Kubernetes?

options.text: Process, file, and network events enriched with namespace and pod metadata
Only CPU and memory utilization metrics
Only the list of installed packages
Screenshots of the application UI

questions.explanation: Runtime protection captures process, file, and network events with namespace and pod metadata, enabling behavioral detection and investigation with context.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Events + Kubernetes context is the foundation of runtime detection.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Resource metrics are observability, not security.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Installed packages are a static view (image assessment).
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Screenshots are not part of the telemetry.

questions.memory_tip: Runtime: process + file + network, with namespace/pod.

questions.question: What does the principle of least privilege mean in cloud identity management?

options.text: Granting an identity only the minimum permissions necessary to perform its role
Granting broad access to avoid blocking team members
Removing all permissions from all users
Using a single shared admin account for the whole team

questions.explanation: Least privilege: each identity receives only the minimum permissions for its function, reducing the attack surface.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Minimum necessary for the function.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Broad access for convenience is exactly the risk CIEM fights.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. With no permissions, no one can work; the principle is balance.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. A shared account violates traceability and least privilege.

questions.memory_tip: Least privilege: only what's needed, nothing more.

questions.question: Why is managing identities across AWS, Azure, and GCP particularly challenging, motivating the use of CIEM?

options.text: Each provider has a different IAM model, making unified visibility and governance difficult
Cloud providers do not offer any identity controls
Identities in the cloud never change
Only one provider can be used at a time

questions.explanation: AWS, Azure, and GCP have different IAM models. With hundreds of resources and entities, CIEM centralizes visibility and governance of entitlements.

option_explanations.A: Correct. IAM fragmentation across clouds requires a unified view.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. All of them offer IAM; the problem is heterogeneity.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Identities and permissions change constantly.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Multi-cloud is precisely the common scenario.

questions.memory_tip: 3 clouds, 3 different IAMs: CIEM unifies.

questions.question: Which capabilities does a CIEM solution provide for cloud identities?

options.text: Analyzes effective access, right-sizes permissions, detects accidental exposure, and generates remediation recommendations
Encrypts all data at rest
Provides DDoS protection for public endpoints
Manages DNS records for cloud domains

questions.explanation: CIEM uses analytics (including machine learning) to understand effective access, right-size permissions to what's needed, detect accidental exposure, and recommend fixes.

option_explanations.A: Correct. This is the core set of CIEM capabilities.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Encryption is data control, not entitlements.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. DDoS protection is network protection.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. DNS is not part of identity's scope.

questions.memory_tip: CIEM: see real access, shrink permissions, fix exposure.

questions.question: CrowdStrike observed cloud intrusion breakout times as fast as under 30 seconds. What does this imply for cloud detection and response?

options.text: Detection must happen in real time on the control plane; daily batch reviews are insufficient
Weekly posture reports are enough to stop intrusions
Adversaries take days to move laterally in the cloud
Response time does not matter in cloud environments

questions.explanation: With breakout in seconds, detection needs to be real time on the control plane; periodic reviews arrive too late to contain the adversary.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Attack speed demands real-time detection and response.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Weekly reports are posture, not intrusion response.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The data shows the opposite: minutes or seconds.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Response time is the decisive factor.

questions.memory_tip: Breakout in seconds: real-time detection or nothing.

questions.question: In AWS, which log source feeds the control plane activity that Falcon Cloud Security uses to generate cloud IOAs?

options.text: CloudTrail management events
S3 server access logs only
EC2 instance system logs
Billing and cost reports

questions.explanation: AWS cloud IOAs are based on CloudTrail management events, which record control plane API calls in each region.

option_explanations.A: Correct. CloudTrail management events are the control plane's source.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. S3 access logs only cover one service's data.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Instance logs are workload, not control plane.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Billing does not record security API activity.

questions.memory_tip: AWS control plane = CloudTrail management events.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Falcon Shield?

options.text: SaaS security posture management: finding misconfigurations, risky identities, and threats across the organization's SaaS applications
Scanning container images in registries
Protecting on-premises file servers
Managing cloud infrastructure costs

questions.explanation: Falcon Shield is the SSPM solution: it assesses SaaS application posture, finds misconfigurations, risky identities, and threats across the SaaS stack.

option_explanations.A: Correct. SSPM for the organization's SaaS stack.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Container images are Falcon Cloud Security's scope.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. On-prem file servers are endpoint, not SaaS.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Cost is not part of Shield's scope.

questions.memory_tip: Shield = SSPM: posture of your SaaS stack.

questions.question: How does Falcon Shield evaluate the configuration of each connected SaaS application?

options.text: It runs automated security checks against industry benchmarks and company policies, assigning pass/fail results and app-specific security scores
It requires manual review of every setting by an administrator
It only checks whether the app is licensed correctly
It disables all integrations by default

questions.explanation: Shield automates thousands of checks against benchmarks and policies, assigns pass/fail results and a score per app to prioritize the highest-risk apps.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Automated checks + scores per app.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. SSPM's value is precisely automating the review.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Licensing is not security posture.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Shield monitors integrations, it does not disable them by default.

questions.memory_tip: Shield: automated check, pass/fail, score per app.

questions.question: A SaaS admin changes a security setting in one of the company's applications. How does Falcon Shield help the security team respond?

options.text: It alerts on the configuration drift as it happens, showing the change, its risk, and which users are impacted
It silently reverts every change
It blocks all admin logins to the application
It waits for the next quarterly review to report it

questions.explanation: Shield alerts on configuration drift in real time, letting the team review the change, assess the risk, and see the impacted users before acting.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Drift alert with risk context and users.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Silent reversal is not the behavior.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Blocking logins would be a drastic action, not monitoring.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The alert is immediate, not quarterly.

questions.memory_tip: Config changed in the SaaS app? Shield alerts on the drift right away.

questions.question: Beyond misconfigurations, which identity risks does Falcon Shield surface across SaaS applications?

options.text: Human and non-human identities that are over-permissioned, dormant, high risk, or partially deprovisioned
Only users who forgot their passwords
Only identities in the corporate VPN
Printer and IoT device certificates

questions.explanation: Shield identifies human and non-human identities (integrations, tokens, service accounts) with excessive permissions, that are dormant, or poorly deprovisioned.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Coverage of human and non-human identities in SaaS.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. A forgotten password is a helpdesk matter, not a posture risk.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The scope is the SaaS stack, not the VPN.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Device certificates are not SSPM's focus.

questions.memory_tip: Shield looks at human AND non-human identities in SaaS.

questions.question: What does Application Security Posture Management (ASPM) provide in Falcon Cloud Security?

options.text: Continuous visibility and risk management of applications from code to runtime, prioritized by business impact
A replacement for all developer testing
Antivirus signatures for mobile apps
Automatic rewriting of vulnerable source code

questions.explanation: ASPM gives continuous visibility into application posture from code to runtime, correlating signals and prioritizing risk by business impact.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Code to runtime, prioritized by business impact.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. ASPM complements testing, it does not replace it.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Antivirus signatures are not the mechanism.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. There is no automatic code rewriting.

questions.memory_tip: ASPM: application posture, from code to runtime.

questions.question: How does ASPM reduce vulnerability noise for application security teams?

options.text: By correlating and deduplicating findings and prioritizing vulnerabilities that are actually reachable and business-critical at runtime
By hiding all findings below critical severity
By scanning applications only once a year
By disabling the other security scanners

questions.explanation: ASPM centralizes signals (SAST, DAST, SCA, IaC, runtime), deduplicates them, and prioritizes what is reachable and business-critical, drastically cutting noise.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Correlation + reachability + business criticality.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Hiding severities is not prioritizing risk.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Less frequency = more risk, not less noise.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. ASPM aggregates the scanners rather than disabling them.

questions.memory_tip: ASPM cuts noise: deduplicates and prioritizes what's actually exploitable.

questions.question: Why does ASPM discover and map microservices, databases, APIs, and the software bill of materials (SBOM) of running applications?

options.text: To support compliance and identify software supply chain risk across dependencies
To generate marketing documentation
To increase application latency for testing
To migrate applications between clouds automatically

questions.explanation: Mapping services, APIs, and SBOM reveals dependencies and third-party components, supporting compliance and software supply chain risk management.

option_explanations.A: Correct. SBOM + application map = supply chain visibility.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. It is not for marketing purposes.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Mapping does not intentionally inject latency.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Cloud migration is not an ASPM function.

questions.memory_tip: SBOM: knowing what makes up the application = controlling the supply chain.

questions.question: Which model do Falcon Fusion SOAR workflows follow?

options.text: Trigger, conditions, and actions, built in a no-code interface with conditional branching
Compile, deploy, and rollback
Extract, transform, and load
Plan, do, check, act

questions.explanation: Fusion follows the trigger/condition/action model in a no-code interface, with conditional branching (if/else) and sequential or parallel flows.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Trigger + condition + action, no code.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. That is CI/CD vocabulary, not SOAR.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. ETL is data engineering.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. PDCA is quality management.

questions.memory_tip: Fusion: fires (trigger), evaluates (condition), executes (action).

questions.question: What can start the execution of a Falcon Fusion SOAR workflow? (Select TWO.)

options.text: An alert, detection, or incident generated in the Falcon platform
A defined schedule that runs the workflow regularly
Only a manual phone call to CrowdStrike support
A restart of the cloud provider's hypervisor

questions.explanation: Workflows can be triggered by alerts, detections, and incidents, or run on a defined schedule for recurring tasks.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Platform events are natural triggers.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Scheduling runs recurring workflows.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. There is no dependency on support.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Hypervisor events are not Fusion triggers.

questions.memory_tip: Fusion trigger: platform event or schedule.

questions.question: A SOC wants to automatically contain hosts when a high-severity cloud detection occurs, without analyst intervention. Which capability implements this?

options.text: A Falcon Fusion SOAR workflow triggered by the detection, with a containment action
A weekly CSPM compliance report
An image assessment policy in the registry
A manual runbook printed for the on-call analyst

questions.explanation: A Fusion workflow triggered by the high-severity detection with a containment action automates the immediate response, freeing up analysts.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Trigger on the detection + containment action = automatic response.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. A posture report does not contain real-time threats.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Image policy acts in the pipeline, not in response.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. A manual runbook is the opposite of automation.

questions.memory_tip: Critical detection + automatic containment = a Fusion workflow.
